summary:
Yumuktepe (also known as Mersin-Yumuktepe) is a tell site located on the Mediterranean coast of southern Turkey near the modern city of Mersin, with occupation evidence spanning from approximately 7000 BCE through into the historic period — one of the longest occupation sequences at any site in the Near East. The earliest Neolithic levels at the site provide evidence for farming communities on the Cilician coastal plain during the seventh millennium BCE, connecting the agricultural traditions of the Anatolian interior with the coastal Mediterranean world.

The strategic position of Yumuktepe on the Cilician coast made it a natural point of contact between inland Anatolian communities and the broader Mediterranean world. The site's long occupation sequence documents the gradual development of settlement life in this coastal zone across many successive cultural phases. Early Neolithic levels include evidence of mud-brick architecture, cultivated cereals, herded animals, and simple pottery consistent with the broader Anatolian Neolithic tradition.

Yumuktepe's coastal location gave it access to both marine resources and the overland routes connecting Anatolia with the Levant and Syria, positioning it as part of the exchange network that linked early Neolithic communities across the northern Levant and Cilicia. The site contributes to understanding how coastal Anatolian communities participated in the broader Neolithic cultural sphere of the eastern Mediterranean during this formative period.
